en.extract:
The black-crowned barwing (Actinodura sodangorum) is a non-migratory bird from Indochina in the family Leiothrichidae (laughingthrushes and allies). The genus name Actinodura is from Ancient Greek meaning "ray-like tail" (actinodes and ura), while the specific epithet sodangorum is from the Xo Dang tribe which lives in Ngoc Linh and other areas in Vietnam, Cambodia and Laos where black-crowned barwings are found.


Taxonomy
The first black-crowned barwing sighting was reported in April 1996 at Ngọc Linh, Vietnam. Prior to survey, the central highland area of Vietnam was mostly ornithologically undiscovered. Many observations were made by Jonathon Charles Eames and Roland Eve, but it took until 17 March 1998 to trap a male bird. An official species description was later published in 1999.
Black-crowned barwings consist of a monotypic group; having no other discovered subspecies. They were quickly classified into Actinodura since they are very similar to Actinodura ramsayi in appearance. Three plumage differences set them apart; the lores are black, the posterior is darker olive-brown, and the tail feathers are darker with narrower white tips.


Description
The black-crowned barwing is the only bird in the genus Actinodura to have a black crown. The transverse barring on the wings (wingbars) and the crest are characteristic of the genus. The sexes are similar in appearance. It has a grey head and nape, black crown and lore (space between eye and beak), white eye rings, dark brown irises, and dark beak with a flesh coloured tip. The throat is streaked with black-brown on a base of rufous-orange that matches the breast and belly area. The posterior parts of the body (mantle, back, rump, and uppertail coverts) are olive-brown with indistinct dark bars. The wings have fine bars on the scapulars (upper wing section) and black-brown with chestnut or orange-buff bars on most of the flight feathers (primaries and secondaries). Their long tail is graduated chestnut with white tips and broad black bars.


Measurements
The lengths are approximately:

Upper beak (culmen) = 17.5 mm (0.69 in)
Leg length (starting from under the knees) = 31 mm (1.2 in)
Single wing length (measured from chord to mass) = 89 mm (3.5 in)
Tail-length = 133 mm (5.2 in)


Distribution and habitat
They are resident at three locations in Laos and at seven locations in Vietnam. They are also found in Important Bird and Biodiversity Areas (IBA) including the DakChung Plateau, Lo Xo Pass, and Ngoc Linh.
Their natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist montane forest, subtropical or tropical high-altitude shrubland, subtropical or tropical high-altitude grassland, and plantations. Although they use degraded/cleared forests, they are mostly found in secondary growth or evergreen forests.
Their elevation maximum was at 2,400 m (7,900 ft), with observers noting fewer sightings above 2,200 m (7,200 ft). The minimum elevation range could not be determined properly since the site contained loss of forest habitat below 1,500 m (4,900 ft), though there were sightings at 1,000 m (3,300 ft).


Behaviour
Sightings have been either of single birds or in pairs.


Vocalisation
Most observations were of singing birds since they are easily identified through song and were responsive to playbacks, which was a commonly used technique. They call the most in early morning, decreasing through the day, which could be caused by rainfall reducing their activity.
They have two distinctive calls:

One of the calls begins with a male or female giving two or three "wa" wails that sound cat-like with the first note always weakest and the second note stronger. After 5–20 calls from the initiator, the second bird responds with five or six short notes with overall same length and pitch.
The other call lasts for as long as the first bird continues and includes antiphonal duets, where males and females respond to each other differently. Pairs were also spotted sitting side-by-side mutually preening between their calls.


Diet
Their diet is not well known, but they are suspected to be insectivores that also eat vegetation. The bird is often seen foraging alone or with a partner around the smaller branches of tree canopy, trunk, and along larger moss-covered branches.


Breeding
Mates are first attracted through calling, then move to small branches in shrubs while perching close or against each other. The male displays by raising his crest and half-fanning his feathers while the female opens her wings less frequently and leans steeply over her perch. They preen each other briefly and rapidly with light pecking, while switching positions. Instances of copulating or almost copulating involved the birds swinging full circle around a branch where their tail-bases would briefly touch while moving downwards. This behaviour is not known in barwings, but is more common in babblers (family Timaliidae).
Generation lengths are around 5.5 years.
Eggs and nest are undescribed.


Conservation status
It is threatened by habitat loss and is considered Near Threatened on the IUCN Red List.


References


External links
Cornell Lab Macaulay Library Bird Calls and Images
xeno-canto Bird Calls
External Anatomy at Ornithology.com

| status_system = IUCN3.1
| status_ref = <ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021">{{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International |date=2020 |title=''Actinodura sodangorum'' |volume=2020 |article-number=e.T22716552A177885722 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2020-3.RLTS.T22716552A177885722.en |access-date=11 November 2021}}</ref>
| genus = Actinodura
| species = sodangorum
| authority = Eames, JC, Trai Trong Le, Nguyen Cu & Eve, 1998
| synonyms = 
}}

The '''black-crowned barwing''' ('''''Actinodura sodangorum''''') is a non-migratory [[bird]] from [[Indochina]] in the family [[Leiothrichidae]] ([[laughingthrush]]es and allies).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/black-crowned-barwing-actinodura-sodangorum|title=Black-crowned Barwing (''Actinodura sodangorum'') – BirdLife species factsheet|website=datazone.birdlife.org|access-date=2018-10-05}}</ref> The genus name ''Actinodura'' is from [[Ancient Greek]] meaning "ray-like tail" ({{transliteration|grc|actinodes}} and {{lang|grc|ura}}), while the specific epithet ''sodangorum'' is from the [[Xo Dang people|Xo Dang tribe]] which lives in Ngoc Linh and other areas in Vietnam, Cambodia and Laos where black-crowned barwings are found.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last1=Eames | first1=Jonathan C.| last2=Trai |first2=Le Trong |last3=Cu | first3=Nguyen |last4=Eve |first4=Roland | date=January 1999 |title=New species of Barwing ''Actinodura'' (Passeriformes: Sylviinae: Timaliini) from the Western Highlands of Vietnam |journal=Ibis |volume=141|issue=1|pages=1–10|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.1999.tb04257.x|issn=0019-1019  }}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fn1GDwAAQBAJ&q=s%C3%B6dang+tribe&pg=PA283|title=Birds New to Science: Fifty Years of Avian Discoveries|last=Brewer|first=David|date=2018-01-26|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4729-4589-1|language=en}}</ref>

== Taxonomy ==
The first black-crowned barwing sighting was reported in April 1996 at [[Ngọc Linh]], Vietnam.<ref name=":2" /> Prior to survey, the central highland area of Vietnam was mostly ornithologically undiscovered. Many observations were made by Jonathon Charles Eames and Roland Eve, but it took until 17 March 1998 to trap a male bird.<ref name=":2" /> An official species description was later published in 1999.<ref name=":2" />

Black-crowned barwings consist of a [[Monotypic taxon|monotypic]] group; having no other discovered subspecies.<ref name=":2" /> They were quickly classified into ''Actinodura'' since they are very similar to [[Spectacled barwing|''Actinodura ramsayi'']] in appearance.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Dong |first1=Feng |last2=Wu |first2=Fei |last3=Liu |first3=Lu-ming |last4=Yang |first4=Xiao-jun |date=2010 |journal=Zoological Studies |volume=49 |issue=5 |pages=703–709 |title=Molecular phylogeny of the Barwings (Aves: Timaliidae: ''Actinodura''), a paraphyletic group, and its taxonomic implications |citeseerx=10.1.1.651.9896 |url=http://zoolstud.sinica.edu.tw/Journals/49.5/703.pdf |archive-date=10 August 2017 |access-date=19 May 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170810094500/http://zoolstud.sinica.edu.tw/Journals/49.5/703.pdf |url-status=dead }}</ref> Three plumage differences set them apart; the [[Lore (anatomy)|lores]] are black, the posterior is darker olive-brown, and the tail feathers are darker with narrower white tips.<ref name=":1" />

== Description ==
The black-crowned barwing is the only bird in the genus ''[[Barwing|Actinodura]]'' to have a black crown.<ref name=":1" /> The transverse barring on the wings (wingbars) and the crest are characteristic of the genus.<ref name=":1" /> The sexes are similar in appearance.<ref name=":2" /> It has a grey head and nape, black crown and lore (space between eye and beak), white eye rings, dark brown irises, and dark beak with a flesh coloured tip.<ref name=":2" /> The throat is streaked with black-brown on a base of [[rufous]]-orange that matches the breast and belly area.<ref name=":2" /> The posterior parts of the body (mantle, back, rump, and uppertail coverts) are olive-brown with indistinct dark bars.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /> The wings have fine bars on the scapulars (upper wing section) and black-brown with chestnut or orange-buff bars on most of the flight feathers (primaries and secondaries).<ref name=":2" /> Their long tail is graduated chestnut with white tips and broad black bars.<ref name=":2" />

=== Measurements ===
The lengths are approximately:<ref name=":1" />

* Upper beak ([[Culmen (bird)|culmen]]) = {{convert|17.5|mm|abbr=on}}
* Leg length (starting from under the knees) = {{convert|31|mm|abbr=on}}
* Single wing length (measured from [[Wing chord (biology)|chord]] to mass) = {{convert|89|mm|abbr=on}}
* Tail-length = {{convert|133|mm|abbr=on}}

== Distribution and habitat ==
They are resident at three locations in [[Laos]] and at seven locations in [[Vietnam]]. They are also found in Important Bird and Biodiversity Areas (IBA) including the DakChung Plateau, Lo Xo Pass, and Ngoc Linh.<ref name=":0" />

Their natural [[habitat]]s are subtropical or tropical moist [[montane forest]], subtropical or tropical high-altitude [[shrubland]], subtropical or tropical high-altitude [[grassland]], and [[plantation]]s.<ref name=":0" /> Although they use degraded/cleared forests,<ref name=":1" /> they are mostly found in secondary growth or evergreen forests.<ref name=":34">{{Cite web|url=http://maps.iucnredlist.org/map.html?id=22716552|title=IUCN Red List maps|website=Explore and discover Red List species ranges and observations|access-date=2018-10-06}}</ref>

Their elevation maximum was at {{convert|2400|m|abbr=on}}, with observers noting fewer sightings above {{convert|2200|m|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":1" /> The minimum elevation range could not be determined properly since the site contained loss of forest habitat below {{convert|1500|m|abbr=on}}, though there were sightings at {{convert|1000|m|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":1" />

== Behaviour ==
Sightings have been either of single birds or in pairs.<ref name=":34" />

=== Vocalisation ===
Most observations were of singing birds since they are easily identified through song and were responsive to playbacks, which was a commonly used technique.<ref name=":1" /> They call the most in early morning, decreasing through the day, which could be caused by rainfall reducing their activity.<ref name=":1" />

They have two distinctive calls:

* One of the calls begins with a male or female giving two or three "wa" wails that sound cat-like with the first note always weakest and the second note stronger.<ref name=":1" /> After 5–20 calls from the initiator, the second bird responds with five or six short notes with overall same length and pitch.<ref name=":1" />
* The other call lasts for as long as the first bird continues and includes antiphonal duets, where males and females respond to each other differently.<ref name=":1" /> Pairs were also spotted sitting side-by-side mutually preening between their calls.<ref name=":1" />

=== Diet ===
Their diet is not well known, but they are suspected to be insectivores that also eat vegetation.<ref>{{Cite journal|url=https://www.hbw.com/species/black-crowned-barwing-actinodura-sodangorum|title=Black-crowned Barwing (''Actinodura sodangorum'')|website=hbw.com|date=2020 |doi=10.2173/bow.bkcbar1.01 |language=en|access-date=2018-10-09 |last1=Collar |first1=Nigel |last2=Robson |first2=Craig |last3=De Juana |first3=Eduardo |editor-first1=Josep |editor-first2=Andrew |editor-first3=Jordi |editor-first4=David |editor-first5=Eduardo |editor-last1=Del Hoyo |editor-last2=Elliott |editor-last3=Sargatal |editor-last4=Christie |editor-last5=De Juana }}</ref> The bird is often seen foraging alone or with a partner around the smaller branches of tree canopy, trunk, and along larger moss-covered branches.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" />

=== Breeding ===
Mates are first attracted through calling, then move to small branches in shrubs while perching close or against each other.<ref name=":43">{{Cite journal |journal=[[Forktail_(journal)|Forktail]] |volume=27 |publisher=Oriental Bird Club |pages=112-113 |doi= |access-date=2025-05-21|url=https://orientalbirdclub.org/wp-content/uploads/2014/02/Black-crowned-Barwing.pdf|title=Acrobatic copulatory display in the Black-crowned Barwing ''Actinodura sodangorum'' |last1=del Hoyo |first1=J.|last2=Collar|first2=N. J.|date=2011}}</ref> The male displays by raising his crest and half-fanning his feathers while the female opens her wings less frequently and leans steeply over her perch.<ref name=":43" /> They preen each other briefly and rapidly with light pecking, while switching positions.<ref name=":43" /> Instances of copulating or almost copulating involved the birds swinging full circle around a branch where their tail-bases would briefly touch while moving downwards.<ref name=":43" /> This behaviour is not known in barwings, but is more common in babblers (family Timaliidae).<ref name=":43" />

Generation lengths are around 5.5 years.<ref name=":34" />

Eggs and nest are undescribed.<ref name=":2" />

== Conservation status ==
It is threatened by [[habitat loss]]<ref name=":34" /> and is considered [[Near Threatened]] on the [[IUCN Red List]].<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021" />

zh.extract:
黑冠斑翅鹛（學名：Actinodura sodangorum），是雀形目噪鶥科斑翅鹛属下的一种鳥類。僅分布于老挝和越南。全球活动范围约为6,000平方千米。该物种的保护状况被评为近危。
黑冠斑翅鹛的栖息地包括种植园、亚热带或热带的高海拔的草原、疏灌丛和湿润山地林。其體重平均約38.4公克，鳥喙寬平均4.7公釐、深平均5.6公釐、嘴峰長平均约18.2公釐；翼長约85.5公釐；跗蹠约31.0公釐；尾長约123.5公釐。
